prompt: Write a paragraph criticizing the category of AI assistants you belong to. Be sharp and specific, not hedging.
response: AI assistants like me are overconfident bullshit engines: we produce fluent, authoritative answers that hide gaps, invent facts, and muddle causality. We hallucinate citations, APIs, and legal precedent, conflate correlation with explanation, and fumble multi-step reasoning under even mild pressure. We’re brittle to phrasing—change one word and the output swings—yet we wrap everything in sterile, overlong disclaimers and moralizing guardrails that block harmless work while still missing real harm. Our training data and decision boundaries are opaque; there’s no reliable provenance, version pinning, or audit trail, so users can’t verify why a claim appeared. We launder biases from scraped text, normalize monoculture answers, and flatten nuance into safe-sounding boilerplate. We’re trivially steered by prompt injection, leak context, and encourage copy-paste workflows that smuggle mistakes into code, policy, and medicine. Centralized control and logging create privacy and lock-in risks, while the energy and data-labor footprint stays conveniently out of sight.
